title: Inverted maxRate check in cook's updateExchangeRate action makes the maxRate parameter act as a minimum bound instead of a maximum
claim: The require statement at line 1298 checks `(maxRate == 0 || rate > maxRate)` but the intended logic for a rate ceiling is `rate < maxRate`. The `>` operator is directionally inverted, causing the maxRate parameter to reject rates that are below it rather than above it. This means a user who sets maxRate = X expecting to prevent borrowing when the exchange rate exceeds X will instead be prevented from borrowing when the rate is below X, and permitted to borrow when the rate is above X.
impact: Any user or integrator contract that relies on the maxRate parameter as a safety ceiling gets the opposite behavior. If the exchange rate spikes (e.g. due to oracle latency or collateral price crash), a user expecting maxRate to block the transaction will instead have the transaction succeed, forcing them to borrow at a catastrophically unfavorable rate. This can lead to immediate undercollateralization and loss of funds. Conversely, users are blocked from transacting at favorable low rates if they set any maxRate at all.
paths: 1. User calls cook() with ACTION_UPDATE_EXCHANGE_RATE, setting maxRate=500000000000000000 (0.5e18) to cap the exchange rate at 0.5 xSUSHI per MIM.
2. The oracle returns rate=3576216362941343422 (3.576e18) — 7x higher than the intended cap.
3. Buggy check evaluates: (maxRate==0 || rate>maxRate) = (false || 3.576e18>0.5e18) = true — the check PASSES despite rate massively exceeding the intended cap.
4. Transaction succeeds and subsequent borrow/liquidate actions execute at the dangerously high exchange rate.
5. Correct check rate<maxRate would have evaluated 3.576e18<0.5e18 = false, reverting and protecting the user.

title: Master contract can be initialized as a live cauldron via cook's _call function bypassing the only-clone invariant
claim: The _call function (line 1233) blocks calls to `address(bentoBox)` and `address(this)` but does not block calls to the masterContract (0x4a9Cb5D0B755275Fd188f87c0A8DF531B0C7c7D2). The master contract's `collateral` storage slot is 0x0 (verified on-chain), so its init() function (line 993) can be called by anyone through cook's ACTION_CALL. This allows an attacker to transform the code-template master contract into a live cauldron with attacker-chosen collateral and oracle, violating the intended design where the master contract remains uninitialized.
impact: While this does not directly drain funds from clones, it breaks the protocol's architecture invariant and could lead to: (1) confusion where the master contract appears as a legitimate cauldron, (2) potential griefing if the master contract is initialized with parameters that interfere with administrative functions, (3) the master contract's owner-only functions are callable by the actual owner (0x085e...), which combined with an initialized master could cause unintended side effects on the BentoBox since the master shares the same BentoBox.
paths: 1. Attacker calls cook() with ACTION_CALL targeting masterContract address 0x4a9Cb5D0B755275Fd188f87c0A8DF531B0C7c7D2.
2. callee != address(bentoBox) passes (master ≠ bentoBox); callee != address(this) passes (master ≠ clone).
3. The call executes masterContract.init(attackerData) which succeeds because masterContract.collateral() == address(0).
4. The master contract is now initialized with attacker-chosen (collateral, oracle, oracleData), making it a live cauldron.
5. Verified on-chain: cast call 0x4a9Cb5D0B755275Fd188f87c0A8DF531B0C7c7D2 'collateral()' returns 0x0, confirming init() is callable.

title: Extremely stale exchange rate (70+ days, 13.5x divergence) enables permissionless mass liquidation of all borrowers
claim: The exchange rate stored in the cauldron (slot 10) is 263638998750322911 (~0.2636e18), last updated at timestamp 1775052587 (~70 days ago). The current Chainlink oracle rate is 3576216362941343422 (~3.576e18) — a 13.56x divergence. This means the collateral asset (xSUSHI) has lost ~92.6% of its value since the last oracle update, yet the cauldron still values collateral at the old, much higher price. Under the current real rate, all borrowers with 75% LTV at the old rate are now at ~5.5% effective LTV — massively insolvent. Anyone can call the public updateExchangeRate() to update to the real rate, then immediately call the public liquidate() to seize all collateral at a 12.5% discount.
impact: All active borrowers (totalBorrow.elastic = 1.58 MIM) are severely undercollateralized and can be liquidated in a single transaction by anyone who calls updateExchangeRate() followed by liquidate(). The liquidator would receive xSUSHI collateral at a 12.5% discount. While the total borrow is currently small (~$1.58), this demonstrates a systemic fragility: the protocol has no keeper network or automatic oracle update mechanism, meaning liquidations only occur when someone manually triggers them. During the 70-day window, the protocol continued to use dangerously stale pricing, exposing lenders to bad debt risk.
paths: 1. Attacker calls updateExchangeRate() — oracle returns (true, 3576216362941343422), updating storage from 0.2636e18 to 3.576e18.
2. Attacker calls liquidate(users, maxBorrowParts, attacker_address, ISwapper(0)) — for each borrower, _isSolvent() now evaluates with the new 13.5x higher rate, making all borrowers insolvent.
3. Collateral is seized at borrowAmount * 1.125 * exchangeRate / 1e18, transferred to attacker's address.
4. Attacker provides the MIM shares to repay the borrow, netting the 12.5% liquidation bonus on all positions.
5. Optimal parameter: since totalBorrow is only ~1.58 MIM, profit is limited, but the attack vector exists and scales with borrow volume.
